edge: Anomaly detection models are learned using normal samples in an unsupervised way, which may result in ambiguous decision boundary and insufficient discriminability.
Utilizing a few known anomalies during training may cause another issue that the model may be biased by those known anomalies and fail to generalize to unseen anomalies.
Model may be biased by known anomalies and fail to generalize to unseen anomalies.
We learn AD models using a few anomalies with the objective to detect both the seen and unseen anomalies.

node_2: explicit boundary guided semi-push-pull contrastive learning mechanism
edge: We propose a novel explicit boundary guided semi-push-pull contrastive learning mechanism, which can enhance model's discriminability while mitigating the bias issue.
We find an explicit and compact separating boundary as the guidance for further feature learning.
The bias problem caused by a few known anomalies can be alleviated.
A boundary guided semi-push-pull loss is developed to pull the normal features together while pushing the abnormal features apart from the separating boundary beyond a certain margin region.
Our model can form a more explicit and discriminative decision boundary to distinguish known and unseen anomalies.
The model can form a more explicit and discriminative decision boundary to distinguish known and also unseen anomalies.
Most previous anomaly detection methods are unsupervised and pay much attention to normal samples while inadvertently overlooking anomalies.
Without anomalies, the decision boundaries are generally implicit and not discriminative enough.
The insuffient discriminability issue in unsupervised anomaly detection is due to the lack of knowledge about anomalies.
A few anomalies are usually available in real-world applications, which can be exploited effectively to address or alleviate the insuffient discriminability issue.
Semi-supervised anomaly detection methods focus on those available anomalies to learn knowledge from them.
Anomalies are utilized as negative samples in one-class classification for learning knowledge.

We employ normalizing flow to learn a normalized normal feature distribution and obtain an explicit separating boundary. The obtained explicit separating boundary only relies on the normal distribution.
The explicit separating boundary obtained from a normalized normal feature distribution is controlled by a hyperparameter β, representing the normal boundary.
With the BG-SPP loss, only the normal features whose log-likelihoods are smaller than the boundary are pulled together to form a more compact normal feature distribution.
With the BG-SPP loss, abnormal features whose log-likelihoods are larger than the boundary are pushed apart from the boundary beyond a certain margin region.

node_2: distance from the boundary to the center
edge: We define a position hyperparameter β to control the distance from the boundary to the center.
To make the feature learning more robust, we further introduce a margin hyperparameter τ (e.g., τ = 0.1).
We select the β-th percentile (e.g., β = 1) of sorted normal log-likelihood distribution as the normal boundary bn, which also indicates the upper bound of the normal false positive rate is β%.
We provide hyperparameter sensitivity analysis for β and τ in App. Table 12, it shows that our model is not very sensitive to β and τ.

Anomalies are considered as positive samples in binary classification tasks, but models can overfit known anomalies and fail to generalize to unseen anomalies.

The semi-push-pull mechanism in our method pushes anomalies outside the margin region without enforcing them to deviate greatly from normal distribution, preventing model overfitting.
